questions.scenario: The Orders API only needs to read `orders-db-url`. Which grant is the narrowest healthy starting point?

options.text: Grant Secret Manager Secret Accessor to the runtime service account on the `orders-db-url` secret, if secret-level access fits the service design.
Grant Owner on the whole production project.
Grant Billing Account Administrator because the app reads a secret.
Grant every developer the same runtime access so local tests are easier.

questions.explanation: The role and scope should match the job. Reading one secret does not require broad project or billing power.

questions.scenario: The same Secret Accessor role is granted at the project instead of on one secret. What changed?

options.text: The scope widened, so the principal may be able to read more secrets in the project.
The role became a different service account.
The project stopped being the resource hierarchy.
The secret payload was automatically rotated.

questions.explanation: The role name stayed the same, but the binding scope changed. Scope controls how much of the resource hierarchy the grant covers.

options.text: Organization and folder grants can affect many projects below them.
Project grants can cover many resources in the project.
Resource-level grants can be narrower when the service supports them.
Scope does not matter if the role name sounds safe.

questions.explanation: Scope is part of the access decision. A safe-sounding role can still be too broad if attached too high.
